retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroid Adverse Effects -- Mechanism of Action -- Anti-Inflammatory and Immunosuppressive Effects
retrieved_docs.content: The anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects of glucocorticoids are dose-dependent, with immunosuppressive effects seen mostly at higher doses. The pharmacological anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects of glucocorticoids are extensive and can occur via genomic or non-genomic mechanisms. Most effects of glucocorticoids are via the genomic mechanisms, which takes time, while immediate effects via the non-genomic mechanisms can occur with high doses of glucocorticoids (such as pulse therapy). Clinically, it is not possible to separate these effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cortisol -- Function -- Immune Response
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoids have a number of actions in the immune system. For example, they induce apoptosis of proinflammatory T cells, suppress B cell antibody production, and reduce neutrophil migration during inflammation. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroids -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids produce their effect through multiple pathways. In general, they produce anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects, protein and carbohydrate metabolic effects, water and electrolyte effects, central nervous system effects, and blood cell effects. [1] [2] [7] They have both genomic and nongenomic mechanisms of action. The genomic mechanism of action is mediated through the glucocorticoid receptor, leading to most anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. [2] [5] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- Steroid Use
retrieved_docs.content: Low-dose corticosteroids (eg, hydrocortisone 200 to 300 mg/day) may benefit patients with vasopressor-refractory septic shock. [52] These are not recommended based on cortisol levels or ACTH stimulation testing, as relative adrenal insufficiency is often due to tissue resistance rather than a serum hormone deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroids -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The glucocorticoid receptor is located intracellularly within the cytoplasm and, upon binding, trans-locates rapidly into the nucleus, where it affects gene transcription and causes inhibition of gene expression and translation for inflammatory leukocytes and structural cells such as epithelium. [1] [2] [7] This action leads to a reduction in proinflammatory cytokines, chemokines, cell adhesion molecules, and other enzymes involved in the inflammatory response. [2] The non-genomic mechanism occurs more rapidly and is mediated through interactions between the intracellular glucocorticoid receptor or a membrane-bound glucocorticoid receptor. [1] Within seconds to minutes of receptor activation, a cascade of effects is set off, including inhibition of phospholipase A2, which is critical for producing inflammatory cytokines, impairing release of arachidonic acid, and regulation of apoptosis in thymocytes. [1] [7] Corticosteroids at high concentrations will also inhibit the production of B cells and T cells. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Topical Corticosteroids -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The anti-inflammatory effects of topical corticosteroids include vasoconstriction, inhibition of phospholipase A2 release, and a direct inhibitory action on DNA and inflammatory transcription factors. [14] [15] Vasoconstriction of the blood vessels within the upper dermis decreases the number of inflammatory mediators delivered to the region. [14] The anti-inflammatory effect is also attributed to the synthesis of lipocortin, which inhibits phospholipase A2, thereby reducing the production of prostaglandins and leukotrienes. [15] Topical corticosteroids also act directly at the DNA level to increase the expression of anti-inflammatory genes and indirectly inhibit inflammatory transcription factors, such as NF-kB, to decrease the expression of pro-inflammatory genes. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroids -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: 8. Immunosuppression: The desired immune-suppressing and anti-inflammatory effects of corticosteroids can also predispose patients to infection. A meta-analysis of 2000 patients found that the infection rate is significantly higher in patients using systemic corticosteroids when the daily dose was 10 mg/day. The immunosuppressive effect is impacted not just by the dosage but also by the patient's age, underlying disorders, and any concomitant use of biologic or non-biologic disease-modifying anti-rheumatic drugs. In particular, patients on corticosteroids are susceptible to invasive fungal and viral infections. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Inflammation -- History and Physical -- Risk Factors Associated with Chronic Inflammation
retrieved_docs.content: Low Sex Hormones: Studies show that sex hormones like testosterone and estrogen can suppress the production and secretion of several pro-inflammatory markers and it has been observed that maintaining sex hormone levels reduces the risk of several inflammatory diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: Methylprednisolone -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: As a corticosteroid, methylprednisolone also suppresses the synthesis of cyclooxygenase (COX)-2, an enzyme that produces prostaglandins in damaged tissue and contributes to the inflammation cascade. [44] Through these actions, methylprednisolone can reduce or prevent inflammation by reversing capillary permeability, suppressing the migration of fibroblasts and polymorphonuclear leukocytes, controlling the rate of protein synthesis, and stabilizing lysosomes at the cellular level.

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroid Adverse Effects -- Mechanism of Action -- Non-Genomic Mechanisms
retrieved_docs.content: The immediate effects of high dose-glucocorticoids are mediated via non-genomic mechanisms. At high doses, glucocorticoids bind the membrane-associated glucocorticoid receptors on target cells such as T-lymphocytes, resulting in impairment of receptor signaling and immune response of the T lymphocytes. High-dose glucocorticoids also interact with the cycling of calcium and sodium across the cell membrane resulting in a rapid decrease in inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Inflammation -- Treatment / Management -- Conventional Drugs that Combat Chronic Inflammation
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids also prevent several mechanisms involved in inflammation. Glucocorticoids are prescribed for several inflammatory conditions including inflammatory arthritis, systemic lupus, sarcoidosis, and asthma.

retrieved_docs.title: Prednisone -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Steroids like prednisone reduce inflammation by inhibiting phospholipase A2 (PLA2), an enzyme that releases arachidonic acid from cell membranes. This prevents the production of pro-inflammatory mediators like prostaglandins and leukotrienes. [12] Prednisone reduces inflammation by suppressing the migration of polymorphonuclear leukocytes and reversing increased capillary permeability. Prednisone also suppresses the immune system by decreasing the activity and volume of the immune system. The antineoplastic effects may correlate with inhibiting glucose transport, phosphorylation, or induction of cell death in immature lymphocytes. Prednisone may also demonstrate antiemetic effects by blocking the emetic center's cerebral innervation via prostaglandin inhibition. Prednisone is a prodrug to prednisolone, which mediates its glucocorticoid effects. [13] Prednisone is a synthetic glucocorticoid with anti-inflammatory and immunomodulating properties.

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroid Adverse Effects -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the anti-inflammatory, anti-proliferative, and immunosuppressive actions of corticosteroids. Summarize the monitoring required for corticosteroid therapy. Describe the possible adverse effects of corticosteroid therapy. Explain the importance of improving care coordination among the interprofessional team to enhance the delivery of care to patients requiring corticosteroids. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroid Adverse Effects -- Mechanism of Action -- Effects on the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal (HPA) Axis
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoids exert negative feedback effects on the HPA axis. They directly suppress adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) and corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) secretion. Additionally, by suppressing the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines that stimulate ACTH and CRP secretion, glucocorticoids further suppress ACTH and CRH secretion indirectly in inflammatory diseases. Chronic HPA axis suppression by glucocorticoids leads to functional adrenal atrophy (sparing the mineralocorticoid producing outer adrenal cortex that is functionally independent of ACTH). The risk of this functional adrenal atrophy and insufficiency is challenging to predict and varies from patient to patient but is largely dependant on the dose and duration of glucocorticoid therapy. The adrenal function generally recovers by slow tapering of glucocorticoids.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Inflammation -- Treatment / Management -- Conventional Drugs that Combat Chronic Inflammation
retrieved_docs.content: Statins are anti-inflammatory as they reduce multiple circulating and cellular biomediators of inflammation. This pleiotropic effect appears to contribute in part to the reduction in cardiovascular events.

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroid Adverse Effects -- Mechanism of Action -- Non-Genomic Mechanisms
retrieved_docs.content: By altering the cytokine production via the genomic and non-genomic mechanisms, glucocorticoids lead to suppression of the immune system and decreased inflammation. They target a wide variety of cells, including T-lymphocytes, macrophages, fibroblasts, neutrophils, eosinophils, and basophils. Notably, glucocorticoids have almost no effect on B-cell function and immunoglobulin production. The downstream effects of glucocorticoids are summarized below:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1817
retrieved_docs.content: Prednisone, rimexolone, and paramethasone are all members of the class of systemic glucocorticoids. Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a group of steroid medications that are administered throughout the body to exert anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. By being part of this class, each of these agents shares the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoids, including modulation of gene expression, reduction of cytokine production, and suppression of immune cell activity, which are leveraged in the treatment of a wide range of inflammatory and autoimmune conditions. Understanding that prednisone, rimexolone, and paramethasone belong to the systemic glucocorticoid category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and apply consistent monitoring strategies across these related drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2467
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone, identified as “triamcinolon,” is classified within the group of moderately potent corticosteroids that are used in various combination formulations (“Corticosteroids, moderately potent, other combinations”). As a member of this corticosteroid class, triamcinolone possesses anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties that make it a therapeutic option for managing multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). By recognizing triamcinolone’s place among moderately potent corticosteroids, clinicians can better understand its mechanism of action, appropriate dosing, and potential side‑effect profile when it is employed to reduce inflammation and disease activity in patients with multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1666
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑inflammatory steroids and dehydroepiandrosterone represent separate classes of steroid compounds; anti‑inflammatory steroids are not a subtype of dehydroepiandrosterone. While anti‑inflammatory steroids are synthetic glucocorticoids used therapeutically to suppress inflammation, dehydroepiandrosterone is an endogenous adrenal hormone with distinct metabolic and hormonal functions. Recognizing that these agents belong to different steroid families is important for accurate classification and appropriate clinical use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a class of corticosteroid drugs that exert broad anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive actions throughout the body. Both prednison and rimexolone are individual agents that belong to this systemic glucocorticoid class, sharing the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoid receptor activation, suppression of cytokine production, and modulation of immune cell function. Recognizing prednison and rimexolone as members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians understand their therapeutic indications, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, which are characteristic of systemic glucocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1598
retrieved_docs.content: Antiflogistika, a class of anti‑inflammatory agents, encompasses several corticosteroid drugs that share the common function of reducing inflammation in various clinical settings. Deflazacort, prednison, fluocinonid, diflorasone, halcinonid, and triamcinolonacetonid are all members of the antiflogistika group. Each of these compounds exerts anti‑inflammatory effects through glucocorticoid receptor activation, leading to suppression of pro‑inflammatory cytokines and immune cell activity. Recognizing that these medications belong to the same therapeutic class helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑inflammatory therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and manage dosing strategies across conditions such as allergic reactions, autoimmune disorders, and dermatologic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2761
retrieved_docs.content: Deflazacort, clocortolone, diflorasone, and klobetasol are all classified as antiflogistika, meaning they are anti‑inflammatory agents typically belonging to the corticosteroid family. As antiflogistika, each of these drugs exerts potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects by binding to glucocorticoid receptors, thereby inhibiting the synthesis of inflammatory mediators such as prostaglandins and cytokines. Deflazacort is often used for chronic inflammatory diseases and certain autoimmune conditions, while clocortolone and diflorasone are commonly formulated for topical application to treat dermatologic inflammation. Klobetasol, one of the most potent topical corticosteroids, is employed for severe skin disorders requiring strong anti‑inflammatory action. Recognizing that all four agents share the same therapeutic class (antiflogistika) helps clinicians select the appropriate formulation and potency for specific inflammatory conditions, optimize patient outcomes, and monitor for class‑related adverse effects such as skin atrophy, hypothalamic‑pituitary‑adrenal axis suppression, and systemic corticosteroid exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 306
retrieved_docs.content: Dexamethasone acetate, a corticosteroid formulation, may be employed in the treatment of septic shock (صدمة إنتانيّة). By providing anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects, dexamethasone acetate can help modulate the excessive systemic inflammatory response that characterizes septic shock, potentially improving hemodynamic stability and patient outcomes when used according to appropriate clinical guidelines.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2214
retrieved_docs.content: NCIt COVID-19 Terminology encompasses a range of concepts relevant to the disease, including the antiretroviral drug ritonavir and the steroidal compound dehydroepiandrosteron. Dehydroepiandrosteron is classified as an anti‑inflammatory steroid, indicating that it belongs to the broader class of anti‑inflammatory steroids and may play a role in modulating inflammatory processes. By incorporating both ritonavir, which targets viral replication, and dehydroepiandrosteron, which can affect inflammation, the NCIt COVID-19 Terminology reflects the dual therapeutic focus on antiviral activity and control of the inflammatory response in the management of COVID‑19.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1971
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoids, Systemic constitute a class of potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive agents, and the drugs prednison, meprednisone and paramethason are members of this systemic glucocorticoid group. As systemic glucocorticoids, these medications share mechanisms of action that involve binding to intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, modulating gene transcription, and reducing the production of pro‑inflammatory cytokines. Consequently, they are employed in the management of a wide range of inflammatory, autoimmune, and allergic conditions, as well as in certain oncologic and endocrine disorders. Understanding that prednison, meprednisone and paramethason belong to the Glucocorticoids, Systemic class helps clinicians anticipate their therapeutic effects, potential side‑effect profiles, and appropriate dosing strategies across diverse patient populations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 427
retrieved_docs.content: Deflazacort is classified within the therapeutic category “Immunological Agents [TC]” and is a member of the broader drug class “Glucocorticoids, Systemic.” Within this systemic glucocorticoid class, deflazacort shares its pharmacologic grouping with other agents such as meprednisone and paramethason, all of which are systemic glucocorticoids used for immunologic and anti‑inflammatory purposes. Recognizing that deflazacort, meprednisone, and paramethason belong to the same systemic glucocorticoid class helps clinicians understand their similar mechanisms of action, therapeutic indications, and potential side‑effect profiles, thereby supporting more informed prescribing and patient‑care decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 378
retrieved_docs.content: Beklomethason and flutikason are both classified as locally acting corticosteroids and belong to the potent (group III) subclass of corticosteroids. As members of these corticosteroid groups, each drug functions as an antiflogistika, meaning they are anti‑inflammatory agents used to reduce inflammation in targeted tissues. The categorization of both beklomethason and flutikason under “Corticosteroids acting locally,” “Corticosteroids, potent (group III),” and “antiflogistika” highlights their shared pharmacologic profile as potent, locally applied anti‑inflammatory corticosteroids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1245
retrieved_docs.content: Meprednisone and paramethason are both classified as systemic glucocorticoids, belonging to the broader category of Glucocorticoids, Systemic. As members of this class, they share the typical anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties of systemic glucocorticoid agents, and their therapeutic use, dosing considerations, and potential adverse effects are understood within the context of systemic glucocorticoid pharmacology. Recognizing that meprednisone and paramethason are systemic glucocorticoids helps clinicians select appropriate treatment strategies and anticipate class‑related side effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1239
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone, a corticosteroid with anti‑inflammatory properties, may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي), highlighting its role in managing inflammatory and autoimmune neurological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 284
retrieved_docs.content: that underscores the clinical relevance of anti‑inflammatory glucocorticoids in treating lung injury. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how potent glucocorticoids—both fluorinated (fluorometholone, fluorometholon, fluokortolon, desoximetason) and non‑fluorinated (betamethasone, beclomethasone, fluticasone)—are organized within therapeutic categories, function as anti‑inflammatory and immunomodulatory agents, and are applied to conditions such as acne, inflammatory skin disease, general inflammation, respiratory inflammation from aspiration, asthma, and COPD, highlighting their importance for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1256
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydroepiandrosteron (DHEA) is recognized both as an immunological adjuvant (adjuvancia imunologická) and as an anti‑inflammatory steroid. This dual classification underscores DHEA’s capacity to enhance immune responses while simultaneously exerting anti‑inflammatory effects, highlighting its potential therapeutic relevance in conditions where modulation of immunity and inflammation is desired.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1630
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a pharmacologic class of corticosteroid agents that are administered throughout the body to exert anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. Representative members of this class include deflazacort, rimexolone, and paramethasone (also referred to as paramethason). By sharing the core glucocorticoid structure, these drugs act on glucocorticoid receptors to modulate gene transcription, reduce cytokine production, and suppress immune cell activation, making them useful in the treatment of autoimmune diseases, allergic reactions, and various inflammatory disorders. Recognizing that deflazacort, rimexolone, and paramethasone are all members of the systemic glucocorticoid category helps clinicians select appropriate agents based on potency, pharmacokinetics, and side‑effect profiles, thereby optimizing patient care.
